_note: Variables. Curriculum sequence follows Foundations of Python Programming (Runestone) ch.2 - Variables, Statements, and Expressions. FoPP is GFDL-licensed, so NO content is copied from it: the topic order is used (topic sequences are not copyrightable), every question below is original. Concepts covered: assignment, reassignment, updating, naming rules, evaluation order.

python.explanation: Assigning again REPLACES the old value. `score` held 10, then 25 overwrote it, so 25 prints. A variable holds one value at a time.

java.explanation: The second line REPLACES the value in `score`. It held 10, now it holds 25. Note the type `int` only appears once — on the declaration.

javascript.explanation: Assigning again REPLACES the old value, so `score` is 25. `let` appears only on the first line — repeating it would redeclare the variable.

cpp.explanation: The second assignment REPLACES the value. `score` held 10, now it holds 25. The type `int` is written once, at the declaration.

python.explanation: The RIGHT side is evaluated first: `count + 3` is 4 + 3 = 7. That result is then stored back into `count`. This 'use the old value to make the new one' pattern is everywhere in programming.

java.explanation: The right side runs first: `count + 3` is 4 + 3 = 7, and 7 is stored back into `count`. (`count += 3` is shorthand for exactly this.)

javascript.explanation: The right side is evaluated first: 4 + 3 = 7, then 7 is stored back into `count`. (`count += 3` is shorthand for the same thing.)

cpp.explanation: The right side runs first: 4 + 3 = 7, then 7 is stored back into `count`. (`count += 3` is shorthand for the same thing.)

python.code:
a = 1
b = 2
a = b
b = a
print(a, b)
python.explanation: This does NOT swap them. `a = b` makes a into 2 — the old value 1 is gone forever. Then `b = a` copies that same 2 back into b. Both end up 2. A real swap needs a third variable to hold the old value (or in Python, `a, b = b, a`).

java.explanation: This does NOT swap them. `a = b` overwrites a with 2 — the old 1 is gone. `b = a` then copies that 2 back. Both are 2. A real swap needs a temp variable to save the old value first.

javascript.explanation: This does NOT swap them. `a = b` overwrites a with 2 — the old 1 is lost. `b = a` copies that 2 back. Both are 2. A real swap needs a temp variable (or `[a, b] = [b, a]`).

cpp.explanation: This does NOT swap them. `a = b` overwrites a with 2 — the old 1 is gone. `b = a` copies that 2 back. Both are 2. A real swap needs a temp variable to hold the old value first.

questions.title: Which variable name is not allowed?

python.code: # Which of these is NOT a valid variable name?
python.prompt: Which one is illegal as a variable name?
python.explanation: A name cannot START with a digit — `2score` is a syntax error. Letters, digits, and underscores are all fine anywhere else, and a leading underscore is allowed.

java.code: // Which of these is NOT a valid variable name?
java.prompt: Which one is illegal as a variable name?
java.explanation: A name cannot START with a digit — `2score` won't compile. Letters, digits, `_`, and `$` are allowed elsewhere, and a leading underscore is legal.

javascript.code: // Which of these is NOT a valid variable name?
javascript.prompt: Which one is illegal as a variable name?
javascript.explanation: A name cannot START with a digit — `2score` is a syntax error. Letters, digits, `_`, and `$` are fine elsewhere, and a leading underscore is legal.

cpp.code: // Which of these is NOT a valid variable name?
cpp.prompt: Which one is illegal as a variable name?
cpp.explanation: A name cannot START with a digit — `2score` won't compile. Letters, digits, and underscores are fine elsewhere, and a leading underscore is legal.

questions.title: Using a variable before it exists

choices: It prints nothing, then sets total to 5
python.explanation: Python runs top to bottom. On line 1 the name `total` doesn't exist yet, so you get a NameError. Assignment must come BEFORE use — order matters.

choices: Compile error: total might not have been initialized / cannot find symbol
java.explanation: Java reads top to bottom too. `total` isn't declared yet on line 1, so it won't compile. Declare a variable before you use it.

javascript.explanation: A `let` variable exists but cannot be READ before its declaration line runs — that gap is the 'temporal dead zone'. Declare before you use.

choices: Compile error: 'total' was not declared in this scope
cpp.explanation: C++ compiles top to bottom. On the `cout` line, `total` hasn't been declared yet, so the compiler rejects it. Declare before you use.

python.code:
x = 2
x = x * 5
x = x - 3
print(x)
python.explanation: Follow it one line at a time: x starts at 2, becomes 2 * 5 = 10, then becomes 10 - 3 = 7. Tracing line by line — writing down the value after each step — is the single most useful debugging habit you can build.

java.explanation: Trace it: x starts at 2, becomes 2 * 5 = 10, then 10 - 3 = 7. Writing down the value after each line is the most useful debugging habit you can build.

javascript.explanation: Trace it: x starts at 2, becomes 2 * 5 = 10, then 10 - 3 = 7. Writing down the value after each line is the most useful debugging habit you can build.

cpp.explanation: Trace it: x starts at 2, becomes 2 * 5 = 10, then 10 - 3 = 7. Writing down the value after each line is the most useful debugging habit you can build.
